question: What is inheritance in Java? Explain with a simple example.
mainConcepts: Inheritance is an OOP mechanism where a new class acquires properties and behaviors of an existing class
The keyword extends is used to inherit from a parent class
The parent/superclass is the class being inherited from
The child/subclass is the class that inherits
Inheritance promotes code reuse and establishes an IS-A relationship
A subclass can override parent methods to provide specific behavior

question: In OOP, how does Java achieve Encapsulation?
mainConcepts: Encapsulation is the bundling of data (fields) and methods that operate on that data into a single class
Data hiding is achieved by making fields private
Public getter and setter methods provide controlled access to private fields
Encapsulation protects internal state from unauthorized access and modification
Access modifiers (private, protected, public, default) control visibility
It promotes modularity and makes code easier to maintain

question: What is the difference between abstract class and interface in Java?
mainConcepts: An abstract class can have both abstract (unimplemented) and concrete (implemented) methods
An interface can only have abstract methods (prior to Java 8) and constants
A class can extend only one abstract class but can implement multiple interfaces
Abstract classes use the extends keyword; interfaces use the implements keyword
Interfaces provide full abstraction; abstract classes provide partial abstraction
Interfaces define a contract; abstract classes provide a base implementation

question: What are the four main pillars of Object-Oriented Programming?
mainConcepts: Encapsulation bundles data and methods, hiding internal details using access modifiers
Inheritance allows a class to acquire properties of another class, promoting code reuse
Polymorphism allows one interface to have multiple forms — methods behave differently based on the object
Abstraction hides complex implementation details and shows only essential features
Together these four pillars form the foundation of OOP design
referenceAnswer:
The four main pillars of Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) are:

1. ENCAPSULATION: The bundling of data (fields) and methods that operate on that data into a single class, while restricting direct access to the fields. Data is hidden behind public methods (getters/setters). Example: Making fields private and providing public methods to access them.

2. INHERITANCE: A mechanism where a new class (subclass) acquires the properties and behaviors of an existing class (superclass). It promotes code reuse and establishes an IS-A relationship. Example: Dog extends Animal.

3. POLYMORPHISM: The ability of an object to take many forms. One interface can be used for different types of objects, and the same method call can produce different behaviors. Two types:
   - Compile-time (method overloading): Same method name, different parameters
   - Runtime (method overriding): Subclass redefines a parent method

4. ABSTRACTION: Hiding complex implementation details and showing only the essential features of an object. Achieved through abstract classes and interfaces. Example: A car driver uses the steering wheel without knowing the internal mechanics.

These four pillars work together to create modular, maintainable, and reusable code. Encapsulation protects data, inheritance promotes reuse, polymorphism provides flexibility, and abstraction simplifies complexity.

question: Differentiate between method overloading and method overriding in Java.
mainConcepts: Method overloading: same class, same method name, different parameter list (compile-time polymorphism)
Method overriding: subclass redefines a parent class method with the same signature (runtime polymorphism)
Overloading does not require inheritance; overriding requires an IS-A relationship
Overloading is resolved at compile time; overriding is resolved at runtime
Overriding uses the @Override annotation for safety
Return types can differ in overloading but must be same or covariant in overriding

question: Explain polymorphism in Java.
mainConcepts: Polymorphism means "many forms" — the same interface can be used for different underlying data types
Compile-time polymorphism is achieved through method overloading
Runtime polymorphism is achieved through method overriding and dynamic method dispatch
A parent class reference can hold a child class object (upcasting)
The JVM determines at runtime which overridden method to call based on the actual object type
Polymorphism enables writing flexible, extensible, and maintainable code

question: Explain the use of the super keyword in Java.
mainConcepts: super refers to the immediate parent class object
super() calls the parent class constructor — must be the first statement
super.method() calls the parent version of an overridden method
super.field accesses a parent class field hidden by a child class field
Constructor chaining ensures parent class initialization happens before child
If no super() is called, Java automatically inserts super() (no-arg)

question: What is abstraction in Java? How is it implemented?
mainConcepts: Abstraction hides complex implementation details and shows only essential features to the user
It is implemented through abstract classes and interfaces
Abstract classes provide partial abstraction — they can have both abstract and concrete methods
Interfaces provide full abstraction — they define a contract with no implementation (pre-Java 8)
An abstract class cannot be instantiated directly
Abstract methods have no body and must be implemented by subclasses

question: Why is encapsulation considered important in OOP design?
mainConcepts: Encapsulation protects internal object state from unauthorized external access
It allows internal implementation to change without affecting external code
Validation logic in setters prevents invalid data from corrupting object state
It makes debugging easier because all access goes through controlled methods
It promotes loose coupling between components
It supports modularity — each class is a self-contained unit

question: What is the role of the final keyword in Java for classes and methods?
mainConcepts: final class: cannot be extended/inherited (e.g., String class is final)
final method: cannot be overridden by subclasses
final variable: becomes a constant — its value cannot be changed after assignment
final prevents breaking of immutability contracts
final classes ensure security and prevent subclass tampering
final methods preserve the original behavior in inheritance hierarchies

question: Explain with examples how Java supports multiple inheritance using interfaces.
mainConcepts: Java does not support multiple inheritance with classes to avoid the diamond problem
A class can implement multiple interfaces, achieving multiple inheritance of type
Each interface defines a contract that the implementing class must fulfill
From Java 8, interfaces can have default methods, adding complexity
If two interfaces have the same default method, the class must override it to resolve ambiguity
This approach gives the benefits of multiple inheritance without the problems

question: State the difference between a class and an object in Java.
mainConcepts: A class is a blueprint or template; an object is an instance of a class
A class defines properties and behaviors; an object holds actual values
No memory is allocated for fields when a class is declared; memory is allocated when an object is created
Multiple objects can be created from one class, each with its own state
A class is a logical entity; an object is a physical entity in memory
The new keyword creates an object (instantiation)

question: What are getter and setter methods?
mainConcepts: Getter methods (accessors) return the value of a private field
Setter methods (mutators) assign a value to a private field
They are the primary mechanism for implementing encapsulation
Setters can include validation logic before updating the field
Naming convention: getFieldName() and setFieldName()
They provide controlled access to private data

question: How are constructors different from methods in Java?
mainConcepts: A constructor is called when an object is created using the new keyword; methods are called after creation
A constructor has the same name as the class; methods can have any name
Constructors have no return type (not even void); methods must have a return type
A constructor initializes a new object; methods perform operations on existing objects
Java provides a default no-arg constructor if none is defined
Constructors can be overloaded but not overridden

question: What is the purpose of the this keyword in Java? Give an example.
mainConcepts: this is a reference to the current object — the object on which a method or constructor was invoked
this.field distinguishes instance variables from local variables with the same name
this() calls another constructor in the same class (constructor chaining)
this can be passed as an argument to other methods
this resolves variable shadowing when parameter names match field names
this cannot be used in static contexts
